Malaysia.label: National election cycle Previous and Upcoming Month and Year
Malaysia.value: Election Commission websites - Last elections held in Nov 2022, 5 year cycle - 1.Political crisis ongoing since 2020; political instability caused by coalition or party switching among members of Parliament, combined with the fallout of the COVID-19 pandemic, contributed to the resignation of two prime ministers and the collapse of each of their respective coalition governments since the 2018 general elections.

Malaysia.label: Governance structure - Centralised/Decentralised/Federal
Malaysia.value: Federal. At present, elections in Malaysia exist at two levels: federal level and state level. - The administrative structure of the Federal Government in Malaysia is responsible for administering the entire country covering various areas such as security, finance, education, health and foreign relations.  It is led by the Federal Cabinet headed by the Prime Minister, and assisted by ministries, departments and implementing agencies under it.  Malaysia practices a Parliamentary Democracy system under the administration of a Constitutional Monarchy with His Majesty the Yang di-Pertuan Agong as Head of State.   The Parliamentary Democracy Government System divides the power of governance into three parts:  Executive Power Judicial Power Legislative Power

Malaysia.label: Political stability and continuity   World bank map includes - Voice and accountability, polictical stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption
Malaysia.value: Recent news articles,  Economist Intelligence Unit Political Stability Index,  World Bank Governance Indicators - 1. Malaysia ranked 44 out of 167 countries in the Democracy Index 2024 2. Political stability improved from 2014 to 2024 by ~5 percentage points (67.4 to 72.47)  The political environment can be classified as stable but fragile (Coalition govt) - 1. Malaysia categorised as flawed democracy in the Democracy Index 2024 2. Governance score for various indicators for the year 2024 is as follows -  -Voice and Accountability (The extent to which citizens can participate in the selection of government, their freedom of expression and holding officials accountable) : 54.63 -Political Stability (Measures political motivated violance and/or terrorism) : 72.47 -Regulatory Quality (Ability to implement sound policy and laws) : 65.59 - Rule of Law (Quality of law enforcement) : 62.9  -Control of Corruption (Captures perceptions of extent to which public power could be misused for private gain) : 57.94

Malaysia.label: Government's digital transformation priority in the last 5 years and next decade
Malaysia.value: PM/President recent speech, National Development Plan, Budgets - Same as 2.1.5

Malaysia.label: Institutional capacity for policy implementation
Malaysia.value: World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment - Public Sector Capacity   Malaysia has implemented various strategies and programs to develop digital competencies within the public sector . Key initiatives include the Malaysia Digital Economy Blueprint (MDEB), the Public Sector ICT Strategic Plan, and partnerships with the private sector to provide targeted digital skills training. - National frameworks and programs -  1. Malaysia Digital Economy Blueprint (MyDIGITAL) - Launched in 2021- aims to transform Malaysia into a digitally-driven, high-income nation. A central component is the upskilling of public sector employees to leverage digital technologies effectively in their roles, ensuring efficient and modernized public service delivery  2.Public Sector ICT Strategic Plan (National Digital Department/Jabatan Digital Negara) - enhancing digital literacy and technical skills among government employees.  3. AI Adoption in the Public Sector (Feb 2025) -Public Sector AI Adaptation Guidelines to ensure responsible and ethical AI adoption in government agencies   4. National Talent Framework - Data Science Digital Talent Group which focuses on Big Data Analytics and Artificial Intelligence (AI) - May 2024  5. Public-Private Partnerships -  2024 - Microsoft committed to training 300,000 Malaysians in AI skills  6. Malaysia Digital Workforce Training Directory - directory offers a range of endorsed digital technology courses.  7. State level initiatives -  a. The Selangor State Government - establish multiple AI centres in 2025 b. Johar - emerging as a major AI-powered data centre hub, housing 78.6% of Malaysia’s IT capacity and expected to surpass 1GW in data centre capacity. c. Sarawak -launching an AI Centre to integrate AI into digital infrastructure and drive innovation  Gaps:No uniform mandate requiring all new public employees to undergo training in digital skills

Malaysia.label: Leadership/ Digital transformation champions within the cabinet, PM
Malaysia.value: Quote by   1. YAB Dato' Seri Anwar bin Ibrahim, PM of Malaysia If you want to ensure that an emerging economy succeeds, remains competitive, and sustainable, then it has to be through a quantum leap. And AI is the answer for that, for the matter of digital transformation.  2. YB Gobind Singh Deo, Minister of Digital It is imperative that we bring AI to all sectors, reaching every segment of society – from the young to the elderly in every corner of the nation. This is to ensure no one is left behind and that everyone benefits from this technological shift.

Malaysia.label: Key decision makers and influencers
Malaysia.value: Secretary-General of MOSTI established an AI Governance Structure, consisting of a Steering Committee, a Technical Committee, and five Working Committees on Governance, Legal and Ethics (Working Group 1), Collaboration, Enabler and Innovation Hub (Working Group 2), Talent Development (Working Group 3), Research, Innovation and Data Sharing (Working Group 4) and Acculturation and Communication (Working Group 5).  The Steering Committee’s progress is monitored by the National Digital Economy and Fourth Industrial Revolution Council (MED4IRN), chaired by the Prime Minister. MED4IRN also works to harmonise state-level digital economy plans with national strategies.

Cambodia.label: National election cycle Previous and Upcoming Month and Year
Cambodia.value: Election Commission websites - Previous cycle in 2023-24, next one in 2028-29 - It is a mainly 'one party' model of governance though. Could instead track Key people of Interest and their terms, especially if there is an expected change in MPTC or MISTI.

Cambodia.label: Governance structure - Centralised/Decentralised/Federal
Cambodia.value: Constitutional Monarchy with a Parliamentary system, governed centrally by mostly a One-party rule.

Cambodia.label: Political stability and continuity   World bank map includes - Voice and accountability, polictical stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption
Cambodia.value: Recent news articles,  Economist Intelligence Unit Political Stability Index,  World Bank Governance Indicators - Democracy Index Report 2024 puts Cambodia in the 'Authoritarian' category with a democracy index rank of 123 out of 169

Cambodia.label: Government's digital transformation priority in the last 5 years and next decade
Cambodia.value: PM/President recent speech, National Development Plan, Budgets - Cambodia in collaboration with UN organizations has shown enthusiastic participation when it comes to formulating and enacting digital transformation policies along with making it a priority for the next decade. The National AI Strategy draft is an example of this. - Inauguration of the National Research Center on AI for Education (Nov 2025):  Keeping the UNESCO AI Readiness Report's recommendations in mind, Cambodia has included Capacity Building as a priority in the draft of the National AI Strategy.   Last year, Prime Minister Hun Manet during the Teachers' day address said, 'It resonates with Cambodia's national vision articulated in its Pentagonal Strategy -- Phase I, where human development and digital transformation are identified as two of the core pillars to sustainable growth of the country', emphasizing the importance of the Strategy laid out and its prioritization of Skill Development. - 1. News on the Teachers' Day address

Cambodia.label: Institutional capacity for policy implementation
Cambodia.value: World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment - Statistical Performance Indicators on Cambodia's Data Infrastructure Capacity show increasing capacity for digital transformation policy implementation, while indicators on Political Stability remain mostly flat. - Cambodia from 2019 to 2023 has shown improvement on all DataBank Performance indicators, with Dimensions within these scores on Institutional collection and upkeep of data also increasing. This is in-line with the Digital Transformation policies Cambodia has undertaken in the past few years. - 1. Cambodia Performance Indicators

Cambodia.label: Leadership/ Digital transformation champions within the cabinet, PM
Cambodia.value: Key Champions include the Prime Minister, Minister of Post and Telecommunications, Director General of the General Department of ICT at MPTC, and Deputy Director General of the General Department of Science, Technology, and Innovation at MISTI - 1. H.E. Hun Manet: In 2024, Prime Minister Hun Manet emphasized the future focus on a 'Digital Revolution'.   2. H.E. Chea Vandeth: Minister of Post and Telecommunications has often emphasized a 'balance between ethics and technology', even to student technologists. He has also been instrumental in launching the Digital Skills Roadmap (2024-35)  3. H.E. Sam Sethserey: Director General of the General Department of ICT at MPTC, often recognized as a leader in cybersecurity and digital startup ecosystem development.  4. Seng Molika: Deputy Director General of the General Department of Science, Technology, and Innovation at MISTI, leading the digital transformation unit for the ministry. - 1. PM's Digital Government Forum address  2. Minister's Ethics + Technology statement

Phillipines.label: National election cycle Previous and Upcoming Month and Year
Phillipines.value: Election Commission websites - Six-year presidential terms; midterms every three years. - The Philippines held its National and Local Elections on May 12, 2025, which served as a midterm referendum for the Marcos Jr. administration. The next major transition will be the 2028 Presidential Election. The current period (2026) is marked by the start of 'lame-duck' dynamics as the political focus shifts toward successor maneuvering.

Phillipines.label: Governance structure - Centralised/Decentralised/Federal
Phillipines.value: Unitary state with significant decentralization. - Officially a Unitary Republic, the Philippines operates under the Local Government Code of 1991, which devolves power to Provinces, Cities, and Municipalities. A unique 'Federal-like' exception is the Bangsamoro Autonomous Region (BARMM), which held its first regular parliamentary elections in October 2025.

Phillipines.label: Political stability and continuity   World bank map includes - Voice and accountability, polictical stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption
Phillipines.value: Recent news articles,  Economist Intelligence Unit Political Stability Index,  World Bank Governance Indicators - Stable but prone to elite factionalism.  Mixed performance; moderate regulatory quality. - While the government remains constitutionally stable, 2026 is characterized by an widening rift between the 'Uniteam' alliance (Marcos and Duterte factions). This creates risks for policy continuity if digital initiatives become politicized or if leadership focus is diverted by 'elite intramurals' ahead of 2028.  Voice & Accountability: Relatively high for the region but under pressure.   Political Stability: Low scores (-0.77 in 2024) due to internal conflicts.   Govt. Effectiveness: Improving through digitalization.   Rule of Law / Corruption: Persistent challenges with slow judicial processes and systemic graft.

Phillipines.label: Government's digital transformation priority in the last 5 years and next decade
Phillipines.value: PM/President recent speech, National Development Plan, Budgets - High; shifting from access to 'Digital Economy.' - (2021–2025) focused on connectivity and infrastructure (e.g., Common Tower Policy). (2026–2035) focuses on the Konektadong Pinoy Act and the e-Governance Act, aiming to raise the digital economy to 12% of GDP by 2026 through cloud-first policies and AI integration.

Phillipines.label: Institutional capacity for policy implementation
Phillipines.value: World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment - Growing but uneven across agencies. - The E-Governance Act (RA 12254), signed in late 2025, mandates a unified digital framework. While central agencies (like DICT or the Central Bank) have high capacity, Local Government Units (LGUs) in rural areas often lack the technical staff and budget to implement these national mandates effectively.

Phillipines.label: Leadership/ Digital transformation champions within the cabinet, PM
Phillipines.value: Led by the President and DICT Secretary. - President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. has positioned himself as the primary champion, frequently highlighting the Digital National ID (PhilSys). Secretary Henry Rhoel Aguda (appointed March 2025) is the lead 'champion' at the cabinet level, bringing 35+ years of private sector tech expertise to the DICT.

Phillipines.label: Key decision makers and influencers
Phillipines.value: DICT, NEDA, and the Office of the President. - Key influencers include the Department of Information and Communications Technology (DICT) as the lead implementer, the National Economic and Development Authority (NEDA) for funding, and the Anti-Red Tape Authority (ARTA) which enforces the automation of government services.

Phillipines.label: Technical expertise/ Dedicated authority/staff for digital transformation projects  DIgital readiness of civil service
Phillipines.value: Dedicated ICT agency with specialized units. - The DICT maintains a Central Office and 16 regional offices. Specialized units like the Cybersecurity Bureau and the e-Government Unified Project Management Office (eGov UPMO) lead specific technical rollouts. However, 'brain drain' to the private sector remains a significant challenge for keeping top-tier technical staff.

Phillipines.label: Civil society engagement levels
Phillipines.value: Vibrant but digitally polarized. - The Philippines has a very active civil society. However, engagement is often marred by disinformation and 'troll farms.' While groups like Bayanihan participate in digital literacy programs, PWD and indigenous groups report being frequently left out of high-level digital policy consultations.

Phillipines.label: Overall governance and administrative culture
Phillipines.value: Subjective question - need KIIs - Bureaucratic; shifting toward 'Agile.' - Traditionally characterized by 'red tape' and paper-heavy workflows, the culture is slowly shifting toward 'Digital Bayanihan' (cooperation). The Ease of Doing Business Act has forced a more results-oriented administrative culture, though 'slowly but surely' remains the prevalent pace in many local bureaucracies.

Bangladesh.label: National election cycle Previous and Upcoming Month and Year
Bangladesh.value: Election Commission websites - Elections recently concluded

Bangladesh.label: Governance structure - Centralised/Decentralised/Federal
Bangladesh.value: Centralised - -Bangladesh is a democratic republic with a unicameral parliament called the Jatiya Sangsad -The president heads the state, currently President Abdul Hamid who is indirectly by the members of the parliament - Out of the 300 members of parliament, 50 seats are reserved for women The president appoints the head of the winning party as prime minister, currently Prime Minister Sheikh Hasina  - The prime minister, advised by the president appoint members of the cabinet; currently 45 members - Intergovernmental Profile: Bangladesh – Decentralization Net  Bangladesh

Bangladesh.label: Political stability and continuity   World bank map includes - Voice and accountability, political stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption
Bangladesh.value: Recent news articles,  Economist Intelligence Unit Political Stability Index,  World Bank Governance Indicators - Poor 'D' ranking, with major challenges in corrupution - The Worldwide Governance Indicators analyse 6 areas of governance by relying on surveys and expert knowledge to assess the quality of governments with their potential to implement policy and also taking into consideration, the perception of citizens towards the performance of the state and existing institutions. The aggregates are statistically determined on an absolute 1-100 scale. They can be classified as follows: -Voice and Accountability (The extent to which citizens can participate in the selection of government, their freedom of expression and holding officials accountable) : 36.53 -Political Stability (Measures political motivated violance and/or terrorism) : 40.90 -Government Effectiveness (Quality of public services) : 39.31 -Regulatory Quality (Ability to implement sound policy and laws) : 39.88 - Rule of Law (Quality of law enforcement) : 40.44  -Control of Corruption (Captures perceptions of extent to which public power could be misused for private gain) : 25.55 - World Bank Governance Indicators

Bangladesh.label: Government's digital transformation priority in the last 5 years and next decade
Bangladesh.value: PM/President recent speech, National Development Plan, Budgets - -Has transitioned from Digital Bangladesh (2009-2021) to Smart Bangladesh 2041, the priorities having shifted from foundational connectivity to a secure AI driven digital ecosystem - Aiming to train 20,000 cybersecurity experts by 2027 and 50,000 by 2030 - The ICT Division Roadmap spans over 3 phases; Phase 1, Phase 2 and Phase 3 Phase 1 (2025-26) : Prioritising immediate technological requirements Phase 2 (2027-28) : Scaling and optimisation Phase 3 (2029-30) : Achieving a digital economy - Bangladesh’s digital transformation roadmap draft: Key takeaways | The Daily Star  Bangladesh’s “phygital public infrastructure” bridges DPI theory and practice

Bangladesh.label: Institutional capacity for policy implementation
Bangladesh.value: World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment - CPIA Macroeconomic management rating (1 being the lowest to 6 being the highest) assesses the quality of policy implementation and institutional framework of a country with regards to reducing poverty and supporting sustainable growth - 3 out of 6, as of 2024 - World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment

Nepal.label: National election cycle Previous and Upcoming Month and Year
Nepal.value: Election Commission websites - Previous cycle in 2022 next one in March, 2026 - 5 year election cycle. Mass and violent protests by youth against (popularily referred to as Gen Z protests) took place in September 2025 forcing the resignation of the Prime Minister Khadga Prasad Sharma Oli and desolution of the house of representatives. An interim government headed by former chief justice Sushila Karki has been appointed.

Nepal.label: Governance structure - Centralised/Decentralised/Federal
Nepal.value: Federal - The Federal Democratic Republic of Nepal converted from a monarchy to a democratic republic in 2008, following a Maoist insurgency and eventually adopted a Federal governance structure in 2015. Nepal introduced federalism in 2015 with the aim of addressing three challenges:  - to devolve from a Kathmandu-based Hindu/Bramhin/Kshetri-centred centralised system of government - ensure that development is unilateral across all geographical terrain - increase citizen participation at all levels of the government The governance structure currently established consists of seven administrative regions and a bicameral legislature.  - There exists a president as the ceremonial head of the state, and a council of ministers, under the Prime Minister who becomes the head of government. - Ram Chandra Paudel currently serves as the President of Nepal, while Sushila Karki has been serving as an interim Prime Minister, following the resignation of the previous Prime Minister, K.P. Sharma Oli after the Genz Protests - Long Read: Nepal’s federalist ambiguities - South Asia @ LSE  Nepal

Nepal.label: Political stability and continuity   World bank map includes - Voice and accountability, polictical stability, government effectiveness, regulatory quality, rule of law, control of corruption
Nepal.value: Recent news articles,  Economist Intelligence Unit Political Stability Index,  World Bank Governance Indicators - Unstable. Since transition to democracy in 1990 Nepal has had 27 Prime Ministers. The country has also witnessed two mass protests in 2006 against the Monarchy and in 2025 against government corruption,  discontent with the economy and state of unemployment.  1990- Transition to constitutional monarchy  2008 - Democratic republic - The Worldwide Governance Indicators analyse 6 areas of governance by relying on surveys and expert knowledge to assess the quality of governments with their potential to implement policy and also taking into consideration, the perception of citizens towards the performance of the state and existing institutions. The aggregates are statistically determined on an absolute 1-100 scale. They can be classified as follows: -Voice and Accountability (The extent to which citizens can participate in the selection of government, their freedom of expression and holding officials accountable) : 55.80 -Political Stability (Measures political motivated violance and/or terrorism) : 58.81 -Government Effectiveness (Quality of public services) : 34.14 -Regulatory Quality (Ability to implement sound policy and laws) : 46.15 - Rule of Law (Quality of law enforcement) : 51.74 (Ranked 72 out of 143, as of 2025) -Control of Corruption (Captures perceptions of extent to which public power could be misused for private gain) : 35.05 (Ranked 107 out of 143, as of 2025)

Nepal.value: PM/President recent speech, National Development Plan, Budgets - As in Digital Nepal Framework

Nepal.label: Institutional capacity for policy implementation
Nepal.value: World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment - CPIA Macroeconomic management rating (1 being the lowest to 6 being the highest) assesses the quality of policy implementation and institutional framework of a country with regards to reducing poverty and supporting sustainable growth - 3 out of 6, as of 2024 - World Bank - Country Policy and Institutional Assessment

Nepal.label: Leadership/ Digital transformation champions within the cabinet, PM
Nepal.value: Prime Minister KP Sharma Oli in his previous tenure, had endorsed the Digital Nepal Framework 2019. He has highlighted the need to accelerate good governance, development, and prosperity through the use of ICT. The National AI policy received cabinet approval during his tenure.

Nepal.label: Technical expertise/ Dedicated authority/staff for digital transformation projects  DIgital readiness of civil service
Nepal.value: Nepal scores 0.439 on the GovTech Index reflecting weak systems and skills across government

Nepal.label: Civil society engagement levels
Nepal.value: Active civil society groups that have played a a significant role in the country's political and economic transformation during the past three decades. As of 2019, there were 49,739 NGOs registered with the Social Welfare Council, 19,396 forest user groups, more than 33,000 community organizations registered with the Poverty Alleviation Fund. Think tanks that engage in technology policy also present such as Digital Rights Nepal, Federation of Computer Association Nepal etc
